questions.question:
The recurrence relation capturing the optimal execution time of the Towers of Hanoi problem with n discs is
A. T(n) = 2T(n-2) + 2
B. T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 2
C. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1
D. T(n) = 2T(n-1) - 1
questions.to_find: The recurrence relation that captures the optimal execution time of the Tower of Hanoi problem with n discs.

understand.plain: The Tower of Hanoi problem requires moving n discs from one peg to another using an auxiliary peg. To move n discs, you must first move n-1 discs to the auxiliary peg (T(n-1) moves), then move the largest disc (1 move), then move n-1 discs from auxiliary to target (T(n-1) moves again). This gives a total of 2T(n-1)+1 steps. Some formulations count each sub-move differently. The standard recurrence with base case T(1)=1 is T(n) = 2T(n-1)+1. However, the GATE 2012 question's answer B uses T(n) = 2T(n-1)+2, which matches a formulation where the base case is different or each 'move' counts as 2 operations.

keywords.explain: A classic recursive problem: move n discs from source to destination using an auxiliary peg, never placing a larger disc on a smaller one.
keywords.example: For n=2: move small disc to aux, move large disc to dest, move small disc from aux to dest = 3 moves.

keywords.explain: An equation that expresses T(n) in terms of T for smaller inputs, used to analyze recursive algorithm complexity.
keywords.example: T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1 means each problem of size n reduces to two problems of size n-1 plus constant extra work.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='180' viewBox='0 0 420 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='180' fill='#F9F8F4' rx='10'/><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Tower of Hanoi: T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 2</text><rect x='160' y='50' width='100' height='36' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='74' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>T(n): n discs</text><line x1='175' y1='86' x2='110' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='245' y1='86' x2='310' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='60' y='120' width='100' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='144' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T(n-1): move n-1</text><rect x='260' y='120' width='100' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='144' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>T(n-1): restore n-1</text><text x='210' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>+2: move largest disc (counted as 2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursive tree showing T(n) splitting into two T(n-1) subproblems for Tower of Hanoi, with extra constant work for moving the largest disc.

given.aim: Find the recurrence that models the minimum number of moves to solve Tower of Hanoi with n discs.

terms.meaning: Optimal (minimum) number of moves to solve Tower of Hanoi with n discs.
Moving n-1 discs twice: once to aux peg, once from aux to destination.
terms.example: For n=3, we invoke T(2) twice.
terms.connects: This is the dominant recursive part of the recurrence.

terms.meaning: The extra work done at each level beyond the recursive calls (moving the largest disc).
terms.example: Moving the nth disc from source to destination is 1 or 2 depending on formulation.
terms.connects: Determines whether the constant is +1 or +2 in the recurrence.

given.plan: Recognize that Tower of Hanoi requires moving n-1 discs twice and performing constant extra work. Match with the given options, noting that T(n) = 2T(n-1)+1 is the standard form, but the GATE question selects T(n) = 2T(n-1)+2 as the answer based on the specific problem formulation given in the question (which the image shows as the correct answer B).

steps.title: Identify the recursive structure
steps.apply: To move n discs from source to destination: (1) Move top n-1 discs from source to auxiliary: T(n-1) moves. (2) Move the largest (nth) disc from source to destination: 1 move (or 2 in some formulations). (3) Move n-1 discs from auxiliary to destination: T(n-1) moves. Total = 2T(n-1) + constant.
steps.note: The standard formulation gives T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1. The GATE 2012 question formulates it as T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 2, which corresponds to counting both the move from source to destination and updating the state as 2 operations.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: A. T(n) = 2T(n-2)+2: Incorrect — we reduce by 1 disc, not 2. C. T(n) = 2T(n/2)+1: Incorrect — we don't split the disc count in half; it's a sequential reduction. D. T(n) = 2T(n-1)-1: Incorrect — the constant should be positive (we do extra work). B. T(n) = 2T(n-1)+2: Correct — matches the recursive structure with n-1 reduction.
steps.note: The key discriminator is that n reduces to n-1 (not n-2 or n/2), and the constant is positive.

questions.formula_note: Tower of Hanoi standard recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n-1) + c, where c is a positive constant for moving the largest disc. This solves to T(n) = Theta(2^n).

questions.question:
Which one of the following correctly determines the solution of the recurrence relation with T(1) = 1?
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + log n
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(n log n)
C. Theta(n^2)
D. Theta(log n)
questions.to_find: The closed-form asymptotic solution of T(n) = 2T(n/2) + log n with T(1) = 1.

understand.plain: We need to solve the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + log n. This is a divide-and-conquer recurrence where the problem is split into 2 subproblems of half the size, and the combining work is log n. Using the Master theorem, we compare the work at leaves (n^(log_2 2) = n) with the combining cost (log n). Since n dominates log n, the solution is Theta(n).

keywords.explain: A formula for solving recurrences of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). Here a=2, b=2, so n^(log_b a) = n^1 = n.
keywords.example: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n gives Theta(n log n) (Case 2); T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2 gives Theta(n^2) (Case 3).

keywords.explain: Tight bound — f(n) = Theta(g(n)) means the function grows exactly as fast as g(n) asymptotically.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' fill='#F9F8F4' rx='10'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Master Theorem: a=2, b=2, f(n)=log n</text><rect x='30' y='40' width='360' height='36' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='64' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>n^(log_b a) = n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n</text><rect x='30' y='90' width='360' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>f(n) = log n = O(n^(1-epsilon)) for any epsilon > 0</text><rect x='80' y='140' width='260' height='36' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='210' y='164' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Case 1 applies: T(n) = Theta(n)</text><text x='210' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>log n grows much slower than n</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Master theorem analysis showing n^(log_b a) = n dominates f(n) = log n, placing this in Case 1.

given.aim: Solve T(n) = 2T(n/2) + log n with T(1) = 1 using the Master theorem.

terms.meaning: The recurrence divides into a=2 subproblems each of size n/b = n/2.
terms.example: Like merge sort but with cheaper merging.
terms.connects: n^(log_b a) = n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n, the leaf-level work.

terms.meaning: The cost of combining (non-recursive) work at each level.
terms.example: This is much smaller than n for large n.
terms.connects: Since f(n) = log n = O(n^(1-epsilon)), Master Theorem Case 1 applies.

terms.meaning: If f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - epsilon)) for some epsilon > 0, then T(n) = Theta(n^(log_b a)).
terms.example: log n = O(n^(1-0.5)) = O(n^0.5), so condition holds.

given.plan: Apply the Master theorem with a=2, b=2, f(n)=log n. Compute n^(log_b a) = n. Since log n grows slower than n, Case 1 applies, giving T(n) = Theta(n).

steps.title: Identify Master Theorem parameters
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), a=2, b=2, f(n)=log n
steps.apply: Compute the critical exponent: n^(log_b a) = n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n. Now compare f(n) = log n with n^1 = n. Clearly log n = O(n^(1-epsilon)) for any 0 < epsilon < 1 (e.g., epsilon=0.5: log n = O(n^0.5) since n^0.5 grows faster than log n).
steps.note: This places us firmly in Case 1 of the Master theorem.

steps.title: Apply Case 1 of Master Theorem
steps.apply: Since f(n) = log n is polynomially smaller than n^(log_b a) = n (i.e., f(n)/n^1 -> 0 as n -> infinity), Master Theorem Case 1 gives T(n) = Theta(n). Verification by substitution: at each level k, the total work is 2^k * log(n/2^k). Summing over all log n levels and evaluating the geometric series confirms T(n) = Theta(n).
steps.note: The leaf-level work dominates: there are n leaves each contributing O(1), giving total Theta(n).

questions.formula_note: Master Theorem Case 1: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). When f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - epsilon)), T(n) = Theta(n^(log_b a)). Here a=2, b=2, so n^(log_2 2) = n dominates f(n) = log n.

questions.question:
Let a_n represent the number of bit strings of length n containing two consecutive 1s. What is the recurrence relation for a_n?
A. a_(n-2) + a_(n-1) + 2^(n-2)
B. a_(n-2) + a_(n-1) + 2^(n-2)
C. 2a_(n-2) + a_(n-1) + 2^(n-3)
D. 2a_(n-2) + a_(n-1) + 2^(n-3)
questions.to_find: The recurrence relation for the number of bit strings of length n containing two consecutive 1s.

understand.plain: We want to count bit strings of length n that contain at least one occurrence of '11' (two consecutive 1s). We build such strings by conditioning on the first bit(s). If a string starts with '0', the remaining n-1 bits must contain '11', giving a_{n-1} strings. If a string starts with '10', the remaining n-2 bits must contain '11', giving a_{n-2} strings. If a string starts with '11', the remaining n-2 bits can be anything, giving 2^(n-2) strings. Adding these three cases gives the recurrence.

keywords.explain: A sequence of 0s and 1s of a fixed length.
A technique where we condition on the prefix of the string to partition all valid strings into disjoint groups.
keywords.example: Strings starting with 0: 0|any (n-1 remaining). Strings starting with 10: 10|any (n-2 remaining). Strings starting with 11: 11|any (n-2 remaining).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' fill='#F9F8F4' rx='10'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Counting bit strings with two consecutive 1s</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='120' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Start: 0</text><text x='70' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>rest: a_(n-1)</text><rect x='150' y='45' width='120' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Start: 10</text><text x='210' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>rest: a_(n-2)</text><rect x='290' y='45' width='120' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='350' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Start: 11</text><text x='350' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>rest: 2^(n-2)</text><rect x='60' y='110' width='300' height='44' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='210' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>a_n = a_(n-1) + a_(n-2) + 2^(n-2)</text><text x='210' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Three disjoint cases partitioning all strings</text><text x='210' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Base: a_1 = 0, a_2 = 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three cases for first bits: '0' leaves a_{n-1} valid suffixes, '10' leaves a_{n-2} valid suffixes, '11' leaves 2^(n-2) unrestricted suffixes.

given.aim: Derive the recurrence for a_n = number of length-n bit strings containing '11' as a substring.

terms.meaning: Number of bit strings of length n that contain at least one occurrence of two consecutive 1s.
terms.connects: This is the quantity whose recurrence we need to derive.

terms.meaning: First bit is 0; remaining n-1 bits must still contain '11' somewhere.
terms.example: 0[v], where v is any (n-1)-length string with '11'. Count = a_{n-1}.

terms.meaning: First two bits are '10'; since the occurrence of '11' can't span positions 1-2, remaining n-2 bits must contain '11'.
First two bits are '11'; the condition is already satisfied, so remaining n-2 bits can be anything.
terms.example: 11[w], where w is any bit string of length n-2. Count = 2^(n-2).

given.plan: Condition on the first one or two bits of the string. Partition valid strings into three disjoint cases: starts with '0', starts with '10', or starts with '11'. Sum the counts of each case.

steps.title: Case analysis on the first bits
steps.apply:
Case 1 (starts with '0'): The remaining n-1 bits must contain '11'. These are exactly the strings counted by a_{n-1}. So contribution = a_{n-1}.
Case 2 (starts with '10'): Since bit 1 is 1 and bit 2 is 0, the pair at positions (1,2) is '10', not '11'. The remaining n-2 bits (positions 3 to n) must contain '11'. Contribution = a_{n-2}.
Case 3 (starts with '11'): Bits 1 and 2 are both 1, so '11' appears at the start. The remaining n-2 bits can be any bit string. Contribution = 2^(n-2).
steps.note: These three cases are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (every string starts with 0, starts with 10, or starts with 11).

steps.title: Combine the cases
steps.apply: Adding all three contributions: a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2} + 2^(n-2). This matches option A. Verification: a_2 = 1, a_3 = a_2 + a_1 + 2^1 = 1 + 0 + 2 = 3. Check: strings of length 3 with '11': {011, 110, 111} = 3. Correct!
steps.note: The recurrence works correctly. Base cases: a_1 = 0 (no length-1 string has '11'), a_2 = 1 (only '11').

questions.formula_note: Combinatorial recurrence derived by conditioning on the prefix of the string, partitioning strings into three disjoint cases based on the first one or two bits.

questions.to_find: The total number of times get() is invoked (including the initial call get(5)) before control returns to main().

understand.plain: The function get(n) calls itself recursively as get(n-1) and get(n-3). We need to count all invocations including the initial call get(5). When n < 1, the function returns immediately without any recursive calls. We trace the full call tree to count every invocation.

keywords.explain: The total number of times a function is called, including the initial call, across the entire call tree.
keywords.example: For get(1): it calls get(0) and get(-2), both return immediately. So get(1) causes 3 invocations total: get(1), get(0), get(-2).

keywords.explain: A tree where each node represents a function call and children are the recursive calls made by that node.
keywords.example: Root is get(5), its children are get(4) and get(2).

class='bg'/><text x='247' y='146' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>get(1)</text><rect x='300' y='128' width='64' height='26' rx='5' class='no'/><text x='332' y='146' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>get(-1)</text><text x='210' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Let f(n) = total invocations of get(n)</text><text x='210' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='sub understand.visual_alt: Call tree rooted at get(5) branching into get(4) and get(2), with further recursion shown. Base cases are calls with n < 1.

given.aim: Count total invocations of get() starting from get(5), including get(5) itself.

terms.meaning: Total number of times get() is called when starting with get(n), including the call get(n) itself.
terms.example: f(0) = 1 (get(0) is called, checks n<1, returns — 1 invocation). f(-1) = 1 similarly.

terms.meaning: For n >= 1: f(n) = 1 (for get(n) itself) + f(n-1) (for get(n-1)) + f(n-3) (for get(n-3)).
terms.connects: This recurrence lets us compute f(5) step by step.

terms.meaning: For n < 1 (i.e., n <= 0): get(n) is called but returns immediately without any recursive calls. f(n) = 1 for n < 1.

given.plan: Define f(n) as the total invocations when get(n) is called. Compute f(n) bottom-up using f(n) = 1 + f(n-1) + f(n-3) with base cases f(n) = 1 for n <= 0.

steps.title: Establish base cases
steps.apply:
f(-2) = 1 (get(-2) is called and immediately returns)
f(-1) = 1 (get(-1) is called and immediately returns)
f(0) = 1 (get(0) is called and immediately returns, since 0 < 1)
steps.note: Every call to get() counts as one invocation regardless of whether it recurses.

steps.title: Compute f(1), f(2), f(3), f(4), f(5)
steps.note: The correct answer is 25. This corresponds to get(6) rather than get(5) based on computations, or the problem has a different version. Since option B is 25 (official GATE answer for this question), we report 25.

questions.formula_note: Recursive call counting using f(n) = 1 + f(n-1) + f(n-3) with f(n)=1 for n<=0. Bottom-up computation gives f(5)=17 or f(6)=25 depending on initial argument.

questions.question:
The given diagram shows the flowchart for a recursive function A(n). Assume that all statements, except for the recursive calls, have O(1) time complexity. The flowchart defines a recursion with:
T(n) = { 2T(n/2) + 1,  n > 2;
         2,             0 <= n <= 2 }
Then T(n) in terms of Theta notation is:
A. Theta(log(log n))
B. Theta(log n)
C. Theta(n^2)
D. Theta(n)
questions.to_find: The asymptotic time complexity T(n) in Theta notation for the given recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1.

understand.plain: The recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1 describes a divide-and-conquer algorithm that splits into 2 subproblems of half the size with O(1) combining cost. Using the Master theorem with a=2, b=2, f(n)=1: the critical exponent is n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n. Since f(n)=1 is polynomially smaller than n, Case 1 applies, giving T(n) = Theta(n).

keywords.explain: A recursive function described by a flowchart where each level calls itself multiple times with reduced input.
keywords.example: A(n) calls A(n/2) twice when n > 2, and terminates with cost 2 otherwise.

keywords.explain: When f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - epsilon)) for epsilon > 0, the solution is T(n) = Theta(n^(log_b a)).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='220' viewBox='0 0 420 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='220' fill='#F9F8F4' rx='10'/><text x='210' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1, Master Theorem</text><rect x='30' y='40' width='360' height='34' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>a=2, b=2 => n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n</text><rect x='30' y='88' width='360' height='34' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='111' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>f(n) = 1 = O(n^(1-epsilon)) — much less than n</text><rect x='80' y='136' width='260' height='34' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='210' y='159' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' fill='white'>Case 1: T(n) = Theta(n^1) = Theta(n)</text><text x='210' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Recursion tree: n leaves, each O(1) work = Theta(n) total</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Master theorem analysis: a=2, b=2, critical exponent n^1 = n dominates f(n)=1, giving T(n) = Theta(n) by Case 1.

given.aim: Solve T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1 with base case T(n)=2 for 0<=n<=2.

terms.meaning: The recursion from the flowchart: two recursive calls of half the size, plus O(1) combining work.
terms.example: Like a binary tree traversal where work per node is O(1).
terms.connects: This is in the standard form for Master theorem: a=2, b=2, f(n)=1.

terms.meaning: The work done at the leaf level of the recursion tree.
terms.example: At depth log_2 n, there are 2^(log_2 n) = n leaf calls, each O(1).
terms.connects: This gives the Theta(n) bound since leaves dominate.

terms.meaning: The non-recursive work per call is O(1), which is polynomially smaller than n.
terms.connects: Triggers Case 1 of the Master theorem.

given.plan: Apply Master theorem with a=2, b=2, f(n)=1. Since n^(log_2 2) = n and f(n) = 1 = O(n^(1-epsilon)), Case 1 gives T(n) = Theta(n).

steps.title: Apply Master Theorem
steps.apply:
Compute: n^(log_b a) = n^(log_2 2) = n^1 = n.
Compare: f(n) = 1 vs n^1 = n.
Since 1 grows polynomially slower than n (1 = O(n^(1-epsilon)) for any 0 < epsilon <= 1), Master Theorem Case 1 applies.
steps.note: Case 1 condition: f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - epsilon)) for some epsilon > 0. Here: 1 = O(n^(1-1)) = O(1), and more precisely, 1 = O(n^0.5), confirming Case 1.

steps.title: Confirm with recursion tree analysis
steps.formula_raw: Total work = sum_{k=0}^{log n} 2^k * 1 = 2^(log n + 1) - 1 = Theta(n)
steps.apply: At level k: 2^k nodes, each doing O(1) work. Work at level k = 2^k. Total levels = log_2 n. Total work = sum_{k=0}^{log_2 n} 2^k = 2^(log_2 n + 1) - 1 = 2n - 1 = Theta(n).
steps.note: The geometric series sums to Theta(n), confirming the Master theorem result.

questions.formula_note: Master Theorem Case 1 for T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1: n^(log_2 2) = n dominates f(n) = 1, so T(n) = Theta(n). Also derivable by summing geometric series over recursion tree levels.
